title: Tourtière
subtitle: Canadian Quebec spiced meat pie
excerpt: Tourtière is the meat pie of Quebec: a double-crust lard-and-butter pastry enclosing a filling of pork and beef mince cooked with onion, celery, garlic, and a fragrant blend of cinnamon, allspice, and cloves. It is a Christmas and réveillon pie, eaten on Christmas Eve after midnight Mass, but made throughout winter. The filling is deeply spiced, the pastry flaky from the lard. Serve with pickled beets and mustard.
sourceNotes: Tourtière is native to Quebec and has been made by French-Canadian families since the seventeenth century. The word may derive from the tourte pigeon, now extinct, which was historically the filling. Modern tourtière uses pork; some Quebec regions include beef or veal. The spice blend (cinnamon, cloves, and allspice) is consistent across regional variations. Tourtière appears in 'La Cuisinière Canadienne' (1840) and throughout Quebec culinary literature. It is a food of cultural significance in Quebec, registered as part of the province's intangible cultural heritage.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze assembled and unbaked. Bake from frozen at 180 degrees C fan for 60 to 70 minutes, covering with foil if the top colours too quickly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The filling can be made 2 days ahead and refrigerated. The assembled unbaked pie keeps refrigerated for 1 day.

glossaryTerms.definition: A shortcrust pastry made with lard — rendered pork fat — in place of some or all of the butter. Lard produces a shorter, flakier crust than butter alone because its fat crystals are larger and coat the flour less thoroughly, leaving more steam pockets during baking. The flavour is neutral rather than buttery. Classic in British suet crusts, hot-water-crust, Canadian tourtière, and American pie shells. A half-and-half blend of lard and butter gives both the flakiness of lard and the flavour of butter.

content.text: (half lard, half butter) which gives a short, flaky crust that can take the weight of the filling. The filling is cooked and cooled completely before going in; hot filling steams the raw pastry from inside and makes the base soggy. Vent the top crust before baking so steam can escape; if the steam has nowhere to go, it pushes the top off.

Rub 100 g cold cubed lard and 100 g cold cubed butter into 400 g flour and 0.5 tsp salt until the mixture resembles coarse crumbs with some pea-sized pieces of fat remaining. Add cold water a tablespoon at a time, mixing until the dough just comes together; about 5 to 6 tablespoons. Divide into two portions, one slightly larger. Wrap and refrigerate for at least 60 minutes.

Brown 500 g pork mince and 250 g beef mince in a large pan over medium-high heat until no pink remains. Add the diced onion, celery, and garlic. Cook for 5 minutes. Add 0.5 tsp cinnamon, 0.5 tsp allspice, 0.25 tsp ground cloves, 0.25 tsp nutmeg, 1 tsp salt, 0.5 tsp black pepper, and 150 ml beef stock. Simmer over medium-low heat for 20 minutes until most of the liquid has evaporated. Cool completely.

Heat the oven to 190 degrees C fan. Roll the larger pastry portion to line a 23 cm pie dish, pressing into the edges and leaving a 2 cm overhang. Fill with the cold meat mixture. Roll the second portion for the lid. Brush the pastry overhang with beaten egg, lay the lid over, and press to seal. Trim and crimp the edge. Cut three steam vents in the top. Brush the top with egg. Bake for 40 to 45 minutes until deep golden. Rest for 20 minutes before serving.

items.solution: The filling was added while still warm, or the pastry was too thick. Always cool the filling completely before assembling. Roll the base pastry to about 4 mm — thinner base pastry conducts heat better and crisps faster.

items.problem: Top puffed up and pulled away from the filling
items.solution: Insufficient steam vents. Cut at least three generous slits in the top before baking. If the top is still lifting, weigh it down by placing a small heatproof dish on top for the first 20 minutes.
The pastry was over-worked after adding the water, or too much water was added. Lard pastry should be handled minimally — mix just until the dough holds together and chill before rolling.
